question: How is climate change changing India?

theme.label: Climate, emissions, and air
readerQuestions: Is this about total scale or per-person exposure?
Is the chart annual, cumulative, or a latest snapshot?
What comparison would stop the number from being misread?

plannedCharts.title: How much hotter India is than normal
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single clearest sign that India's climate is shifting, not just its weather.

plannedCharts.title: A century of warming, one stripe per year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The warming trend with no axes to hide behind, the famous climate-stripes view.

plannedCharts.title: Each decade hotter than the last
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Smoothing year-to-year noise into decade averages shows the staircase of warming.

plannedCharts.title: India's actual average temperature, not just the gap
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Anomalies show change; this shows the real temperature India lives in, land-only.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's heat is headed, to 2100
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank CCKP · CMIP6 · observed and projected average temperature
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's warming is small next to where the century is heading, and how hot it gets is still a choice.

plannedCharts.title: It is not just the heat, it is the humidity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Humid heat is what makes warming dangerous for the body; dry heat and wet heat are not the same threat.

plannedCharts.title: How India's rainfall is swinging
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Warming does not just raise heat, it makes rain less predictable, and India runs on the monsoon.

plannedCharts.title: What the air feels like across India today
plannedCharts.subtitle: WAQI · live air quality index · latest reading
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same fossil-fuel burning that warms the planet also fills the air people breathe right now.

plannedCharts.title: ...and how dirty the air has been, year after year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's snapshot means more next to the long run, and India's average exposure sits far above what is considered safe.

plannedCharts.title: The local heat the national average hides: very hot days
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open-Meteo ERA5 · days per year at or above 35°C
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A one-degree national rise lands as many more dangerously hot days in specific cities, which is what people actually feel.

plannedCharts.title: And the nights are not cooling down
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open-Meteo ERA5 · warm nights per year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Hot nights are a hidden killer: without a cool night the body never recovers from daytime heat.

plannedCharts.title: The sea is rising on India's coasts
plannedCharts.subtitle: PSMSL tide gauges · annual mean sea level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India has a 7,500 km coastline and major coastal cities, so sea-level rise is a direct national exposure.

plannedCharts.title: The human toll of climate-linked disasters
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Warming loads the dice on the floods, cyclones, droughts, and heatwaves that kill people.

plannedCharts.title: How much CO2 India now emits each year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: To read India's climate fairly you have to see its part in the cause, not just the effect.

plannedCharts.title: CO2 is not the whole story: the gas mix
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's farms and livestock make methane and nitrous oxide unusually large, so CO2 alone undercounts.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's emissions actually come from
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The path to lower emissions depends entirely on which sectors dominate.

plannedCharts.title: Is India's growth pulling away from its emissions?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether emissions can fall while incomes rise is the whole development-and-climate question.

plannedCharts.title: Is India's electricity getting cleaner?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether the cause is easing depends on how dirty each unit of power is, not just how much is used.

plannedCharts.title: How India makes its electricity, and how it is shifting
plannedCharts.subtitle: Ember · annual electricity generation by source
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The whole climate question for India is whether clean power can grow faster than rising demand.

plannedCharts.title: The clean-energy surge: capacity from near zero
plannedCharts.subtitle: EIA · installed electricity capacity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's solar build-out is one of the fastest energy stories anywhere, and it is recent.

plannedCharts.title: But coal still rules the grid
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The surge is real, but the honest headline is that fossil power still does most of the work.

plannedCharts.title: ...but how much per person, next to others?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Per-person emissions are the fairness number, and they only mean something next to China, the US, and the world.

plannedCharts.title: Total emissions: big, but in proportion
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline 'third largest emitter' needs the company it keeps to be read honestly.

plannedCharts.title: ...and who actually filled the atmosphere?
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Today's warming comes from more than a century of emissions, so historical responsibility is the honest accounting.

plannedCharts.title: Per-person emissions, four very different paths
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The gap is not new; the trajectories show how differently these economies grew.

plannedCharts.title: India's slice of the world's emissions
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Climate is a shared problem, so India's share of the global total frames its responsibility and its leverage.

plannedCharts.title: Why India is so exposed: who works the land
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Heat and erratic rain hit hardest where livelihoods depend on the sky, and a huge share of Indians still farm.

plannedCharts.title: ...and how many people face the hotter century
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The stakes of every degree are measured in people, and India has more of them than almost anywhere.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
